summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Where the child was removed from Australia to the United States of America – Where the father has initiated parenting proceedings in Australia pursuant to the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) – Where the father has also personally instituted proceedings in the USA pursuant to the 1980 Hague Convention on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction – Where the mother claims the father consented to the removal – Where the USA has not ratified or acceded to the 1996 Hague Convention on Jurisdiction, Applicable Law, Recognition, Enforcement and Co‑operation in respect of Parental Responsibility and Measures for the Protection of Children – Where the child’s place of habitual residence is disputed – Where both parties seek that documents produced in these proceedings pursuant to ss 67ZBD and 67ZBE and upon subpoena be used in the US proceedings – Where the Court finds that, as required by r 15.26(1) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth), special circumstances exist to permit the use of the documents in the US proceedings – Where the documents may contribute to the achieving of justice in the US proceedings.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

STRUM J:

Parenting proceedings, pursuant to Pt VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“Act”), in relation to the child, X, born in 2020, were instituted by the applicant father on 2 October 2025. The child was removed by the respondent mother to the United States of America in September 2025.
By his amended Initiating Application filed on 15 October 2025 the father seeks, inter alia, final orders that the mother and he have joint decision-making responsibility in relation to major long-term issues in relation to the child and that the child live with each of them on a week-about basis. By her Response to Initiating Application filed on 11 November 2025, the mother seeks, inter alia, final orders that she have sole decision-making responsibility in relation to major long-term issues in relation to the child, that the child live with her and that she be permitted (post facto) to relocate the child with her to the USA.
The parties do not agree whether the child was removed with the consent of the applicant father, and whether the child remains habitually resident in Australia. That is an important issue, but for another day.
Section 111CD(1)(e) of the Act provides that a court may exercise jurisdiction for a Commonwealth personal protection measure only in relation to a child who is present in a non-Convention country if:
(i) the child is habitually resident in Australia; and

(ii) any of paragraphs 69E(1)(b) to (e) applies to the child; ...
A “Convention country” is defined in s 111CA(1) of the Act to mean “a country, other than Australia, for which the Child Protection Convention has entered into force”, being the Convention on Jurisdiction, Applicable Law, Recognition, Enforcement and Co‑operation in respect of Parental Responsibility and Measures for the Protection of Children signed at The Hague on 19 October 1996 (“Child Protection Convention”).
The United States of America, having only signed but not ratified or acceded to the Child Protection Convention, is therefore not a Convention country.
A “Commonwealth personal protection measure”, relating to a child, is defined in s 111CA(1) of the Act to mean “a measure (within the meaning of the Child Protection Convention) under this Act that is directed to the protection of the person of the child”.
Article 3 of the Child Protection Convention lists the following measures:
a) the attribution, exercise, termination or restriction of parental responsibility, as well as its delegation;

b) rights of custody, including rights relating to the care of the person of the child and, in particular, the right to determine the child's place of residence, as well as rights of access including the right to take a child for a limited period of time to a place other than the child's habitual residence;

c) guardianship, curatorship and analogous institutions;

d) the designation and functions of any person or body having charge of the child's person or property, representing or assisting the child;

e) the placement of the child in a foster family or in institutional care, or the provision of care by kafala or an analogous institution;

f) the supervision by a public authority of the care of a child by any person having charge of the child;

g) the administration, conservation or disposal of the child's property.
A measure within the meaning of the Child Protection Convention under the Act that is directed to the protection of the person of the child has been held to include parenting proceedings.
In Bunyon & Lewis (No 3) [2013] FamCA 888 at [75]–[76], Bennett J said:
In this case the applicant seeks parenting orders about the child. Parenting orders routinely made by our Court include orders dealing with the person or persons with whom a child is to live (s 64B(2)(a)), the time a child is to spend with another person or persons (s 64B(2)(b)), the allocation of parental responsibility (s 64B(2)(c)) and the form of consultation required between persons who share parental responsibility (s 64B(2)(d)), the communication that a child is to have with another person or persons (s 64B(2)(e)) and any aspect of the care, welfare or development of the child or any other aspect of parental responsibility for a child (s 64B(2)(i)). Section 61B of the Act provides that parental responsibility in relation to a child means all the duties, powers, responsibilities which, by law, parents have in relation to children.
It is clear that a Commonwealth personal protection measure, as referred to in s 111CD(1) of the Act for the implementation of the 1996 Convention includes, a parenting order as defined by s 64B(1) and (2) of the Act.
In Duckworth v Jamison [2014] FamCA 308; (2014) 51 Fam LR 471, the mother sought orders, inter alia, in relation to who should have parental responsibility for the child and with whom the child should live. Tree J said at [25]–[26]:
[25] While the phrase “take measures” is not defined in the 1996 Convention, Art 3 provides particular instances of what those measures may comprise, including “the attribution, exercise, termination or restriction of parental responsibility” and “rights of custody, including rights relating to the care of the person of the child and, in particular, the right to determine the child’s place of residence ...” and guardianship.

[26] It is therefore plain that the parenting orders that the mother is seeking are Commonwealth personal protection measures.

(Footnotes omitted)
In Zegna & Zegna [2015] FamCA 340; (2015) 55 Fam LR 134 at [21], Watts J said:
The father’s application for the children to ordinarily live with him in Australia is an application for a Commonwealth personal protection measure as that term is defined in the Act and in the Child Protection Convention.

(Footnotes omitted)
The jurisdiction of this Court, pursuant to s 111CD(1)(e)(i) of the Act, to entertain these proceedings depends upon whether the child is habitually resident in Australia. That threshold issue is presently in dispute and, if these proceedings are to be pursued, will require determination.
It is agreed between the parties that the requirements of s 111CD(1)(e)(ii) are satisfied, namely, that one of the paragraphs of s 69E(1)(b)–(e) applies to the child:
(b) the child is an Australian citizen, or is ordinarily resident in Australia, on the relevant day; or

(c) a parent of the child is an Australian citizen, is ordinarily resident in Australia, or is present in Australia, on the relevant day; or

(d) a party to the proceedings is an Australian citizen, is ordinarily resident in Australia, or is present in Australia, on the relevant day; or

(e) it would be in accordance with a treaty or arrangement in force between Australia and an overseas jurisdiction, or the common law rules of private international law, for the court to exercise jurisdiction in the proceedings.

Since the matter was last before the Court, on 2 December 2025, the father has instituted proceedings in the USA (“US proceedings”) pursuant to the Convention on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction signed at The Hague on 25 October 1980 (“Child Abduction Convention”). I am told that he has done so personally, on advice, and at his own expense, rather than by application of the Australian State Central Authority to the State Central Authority of the USA pursuant to the Child Abduction Convention. I do not understand why that is so, especially when he says, through his solicitor, that he is a man of little means and has already accrued (possibly unnecessarily) considerable legal costs in relation to these proceedings; however, that is a matter for him and lawyers, both here and in the USA.
It is agreed that the proceedings in this Court should be stayed pending the outcome of the US proceedings and that, if necessary, the jurisdictional issue to which I have referred above can be dealt with then.
Material has been produced to the Court both pursuant to orders previously made pursuant to s 67ZBD and s 67ZBE of the Act and upon subpoena. Both parties seek permission to use those documents, and the information therein, for the purpose of the US proceedings.
Rule 15.26(1) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“Rules”) provides that:
The court may grant permission for a person to use a document or information obtained in proceedings for another purpose, including in proceedings before another court or tribunal, if the person can satisfy the court that special circumstances exist to permit the person to use the document or information for that other purpose.

(Emphasis added)
Rule 15.26(2) of the Rules defines a “document” to include, but not be limited to:
(a) a document filed in a proceeding in accordance with these Rules or an order;

(b) a document produced in accordance with a disclosure obligation or a subpoena;

(c) a document admitted into evidence in a proceeding;
(d) a document provided to the court in accordance with section 67ZBD or 67ZBE of the Family Law Act;
(e) a family report or single expert report;

(f) reasons for judgment whether anonymised or not.
The issue of what constitutes special circumstances has been the subject of decisions of this Court, both at first instance and on appeal, and most recently in the decision of the Full Court in Kuang & Kuang [2025] FedCFamC1A 31; (2025) FLC 94-240. If special circumstances exist, my discretion is enlivened.
Principles arising from previously decided cases and referred to and summarised in Kuang & Kuang include:
(a) For “special circumstances” to exist, it is enough that there is a special feature of the case, which is not usually present, which affords a reason for a modification of, or a release from, the restraint.

(b) The “special circumstances” an applicant must demonstrate need only be such as to be “sufficient” to justify a departure from rule; there is no additional requirement that they be “cogent and persuasive”.

(c) The court’s duty is to consider whether the applicant has shown some circumstance which takes the matter out of the ordinary course and, if so, whether an exercise of the court’s discretion in favour of the application be in the interests of justice.

(d) Factors relevant to the exercise of the court’s discretion include the nature of the document; the circumstances under which it came into existence; the attitude of the author of the document and any prejudice the author may sustain; whether the document pre-existed litigation or was created for that purpose and therefore expected to enter the public domain; the nature of the information in the document (in particular whether it contains personal data or commercially sensitive information); the circumstances in which the document came into the hands of the applicant for leave; and, perhaps most important of all, the likely contribution of the document to achieving justice in the second proceeding, that is, whether it has at least the real potential to be important to the proper determination of that case and the administration of justice.

(e) The court ought not embark upon a consideration of the admissibility of the documents in the other court; that is properly a matter for the other court, where different evidentiary provisions may apply. However, the court from which the release is sought can, and will often need to, have regard, more broadly, to the purpose or possible use of that document in the other court; in other words, why the release of the document is sought. Without considering for what purpose an applicant wants to use a document, or how it might be used, it may be difficult to determine whether there is a good reason to release the document from the implied undertaking.

Both parties seek permission to use the documents produced to the Court, and the information therein, for the purpose of the US proceedings. They concede, at least implicitly, that special circumstances exist and urge me to exercise my discretion to permit them to do so. Nevertheless, I must be satisfied thereof.
In the particular circumstances of this case, I am amply satisfied that special circumstances exist, namely, that there is a special feature of the case, which is not usually present and which takes the matter out of the ordinary course, thereby affording a sufficient reason for a modification of, or a release from, the restraint on the use of the documents and the information therein. I refer, in particular, to the interrelationship between these proceedings and the US proceeding, both relating to the child who is the subject thereof.
My discretion having been enlivened, the factor upon which I place the greatest weight is that the documents produced in this Court may contribute to achieving justice in the US proceedings, namely, they have at least the real potential to be important to the proper determination of those proceedings and the administration of justice. Those proceedings relate to the child, who has been removed from the Commonwealth of Australia. The issue of the child’s habitual residence is in issue in these proceedings, for the purposes of s 111CD(1)(e)(i) of the Act and the Child Protection Convention, and it is, I am told, in issue in the US proceedings, for the purposes of the Child Abduction Convention.
Accordingly, I shall order that both parties have permission to use the documents produced to the court pursuant to s 67ZBD and s 67ZBE and upon subpoena, and the information therein, in the US proceedings.
Although the jurisdiction of this Court to entertain the competing applications for final orders in relation to decision-making responsibility for major long-term issues in relation to the child and with which parent the child should live is yet to be determined, I do not consider that a procedural order under r 15.26(1) of the Rules is a Commonwealth personal protection measure as defined by s 111CA(1) of the Act, namely, a measure, within the meaning of the Child Protection Convention, under the Act.
Orders will be made by the Court in accordance with these reasons for judgment and otherwise by consent between the parties.
